content:
{
  "title": "Multilingual Prompt Chaining via Cross-Lingual Semantic Anchors",
  "problem": "Large language models underperform on low-resource languages, especially in cross-lingual transfer and zero-shot scenarios. Existing prompting techniques insufficiently bridge lexical and semantic gaps across diverse languages.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLMs' multilingual capabilities is essential as most languages lack large annotated corpora or high-frequency web presence. Recent advances in LLM prompting focus mainly on high-resource languages, leaving a gap for equitable language technology. Better prompting methods can enable more inclusive NLP and unlock applications in underserved regions.",
  "method": "We propose 'Cross-Lingual Semantic Anchor Prompting' (CLS-Anchor), a novel prompting framework that augments prompts in low-resource target languages by inserting dynamically selected 'semantic anchors'—aligned, high-frequency phrases or concepts from parallel corpora or multilingual embeddings that appear in both high-resource and target languages. These anchors guide the LLM toward more accurate semantic mapping during inference. Prompts are generated by first encoding the task in the target language and then interspersing anchor tokens at key semantic points, preserving grammaticality but injecting cross-lingual semantic structure. This method is model-agnostic and requires no finetuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate CLS-Anchor against standard translation-based prompting and few-shot exemplars on FLORES-101 and XNLI for zero-shot and few-shot tasks, focusing on low-resource languages. Metrics include accuracy and F1 (XNLI) and BLEU (FLORES-101). The strongest baseline is translation-based prompting with back-translation augmentation. Success is defined by statistically significant improvements on at least three low-resource languages."
}
